abstract:
Atomistic simulations are essential tools for fields ranging from drug discovery to materials design. While machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) have emerged as efficient alternatives to expensive ab initio calculations, the scientific community still lacks a general-purpose reactive MLIP—one capable of simulating diverse chemical transformations without the need for case-specific retraining. 

In this work, we present ANI-1xnr, a general reactive MLIP for the C, H, N, and O elements, developed through automated active learning and sampling of condensed-phase reactions. To demonstrate its versatility and accuracy, we apply ANI-1xnr to five distinct chemical challenges: the nucleation of solid-phase carbon, the formation of graphene rings from acetylene, the evaluation of biofuel additives, methane combustion, and the prebiotic synthesis of glycine from small molecules. In each case, ANI-1xnr yields results that closely align with experimental data and traditional high-level modeling methods. Our results establish ANI-1xnr as a robust tool for studying condensed-phase reactive chemistry, enabling high-throughput in silico experimentation across a broad scientific landscape.
